description: Plato absorbed Heraclitean flux for the sensible world, assigning stability only to the Forms.
Plato's Parmenides dialogue engages the Eleatic challenge directly; the Forms echo Parmenidean Being.
Epicurus adopted and modified Democritus's atomism, adding the clinamen (swerve) to account for free will.
Aristotle rejected the theory of separated Forms.
Plotinus reads Plato as teaching the hierarchy of the One, Intellect, and Soul.
Augustine read the Neoplatonists before his conversion; their metaphysics of the One shaped his theology of God.
Aquinas's Thomism is an explicit synthesis of Aristotle's metaphysics, physics, and ethics with Christian theology.
Ockham's nominalism is a direct challenge to Thomistic realism about universals.
Berkeley radicalizes Locke's empiricism by eliminating material substance entirely.
Spinoza adopts Descartes's geometric method and substance metaphysics, then transforms both.
Hume "awoke Kant from his dogmatic slumber."
Rousseau's moral philosophy, especially the general will and respect for persons, shaped Kant's ethics.
Hegel takes the Kantian critical project and argues that the thing-in-itself is an incoherent residue to be dissolved in the Absolute.
Fichte's self-positing I and dialectical method are immediate forerunners of Hegel's dialectic.
Schelling's identity-philosophy is the immediate stimulus the Phenomenology responds to and surpasses.
Schopenhauer accepts the Kantian distinction between phenomena and things-in-themselves, identifying the latter with Will.
Rawls construes his theory as a Kantian account of justice, grounding it in rational autonomy and the categorical imperative.
Kierkegaard's entire project protests the System: it swallows the individual into the universal and leaves no room for the leap of faith.
Marx inverted Hegel's idealist dialectic — the movement of history is driven by material forces, not Spirit.
Nietzsche rejected Hegel's teleological view of history.
Sellars's attack on the Myth of the Given recapitulates Hegel's critique of sense-certainty.
Brandom explicitly draws on Hegel's Phenomenology for his model of recognition and social normativity.
McDowell invokes Hegel's "second nature" to resolve the tension between freedom and nature.
Gadamer's historicism and dialectical model of interpretation inherit Hegelian themes.
Negative Dialectics is in sustained polemic with Hegel's principle of identity.
Nietzsche began as a Schopenhauerian; the will to power is a revision of the Will to Live.
Heidegger read Nietzsche as the culmination and exhaustion of Western metaphysics.
Foucault's genealogical method is explicitly derived from Nietzsche's Genealogy of Morality.
Deleuze's Nietzsche and Philosophy (1962) is one of the most influential interpretations and shapes his own philosophy of difference.
Brentano's concept of intentionality is the starting point for Husserl's phenomenology.
Heidegger was Husserl's assistant; Being and Time transforms phenomenological method into fundamental ontology.
Sartre encountered Husserl's phenomenology in Berlin and it became the method of Being and Nothingness.
Levinas translated Husserl into French and wrote the first French dissertation on his phenomenology.
Gadamer was Heidegger's student; Truth and Method extends Heideggerian hermeneutics.
Derrida's deconstruction is developed largely through a critical reading of Heidegger on language, presence, and metaphysics.
Gadamer engages Dilthey's hermeneutics throughout Truth and Method, both inheriting and criticizing it.
Russell read Frege's Grundgesetze and adopted logicism; Russell's paradox then undermined Frege's system.
Wittgenstein went to Cambridge specifically to work with Russell after reading Frege.
Carnap attended Frege's lectures and his logical syntax program develops Fregean logic.
Dummett's major scholarly project was interpreting and extending Frege's philosophy of language.
Russell supervised Wittgenstein at Cambridge; the Tractatus develops Russell's logical atomism.
The Vienna Circle read the Tractatus as supporting their verificationism.
Anscombe was Wittgenstein's student and literary executor; Intention is a Wittgensteinian work.
Kripke on Rules and Private Language is an interpretation of — and landmark intervention in — the rule-following sections of the Investigations.
"Two Dogmas of Empiricism" is addressed principally at Carnap's analytic-synthetic distinction.
Ayer visited the Vienna Circle and brought logical positivism to a British audience.
Davidson was Quine's student; he inherits naturalism and transforms it with a truth-theoretic semantics.
Quine and Goodman were close colleagues; their joint paper on nominalism is a landmark.
Rorty draws heavily on Davidson's critique of the scheme-content dualism in his neo-pragmatism.
Barcan Marcus's quantified modal logic and direct reference anticipate the core of Naming and Necessity.
Kripke's rigid designators directly influenced Putnam's Twin Earth and natural kind terms.
James acknowledged Peirce as the founder of pragmatism and popularized the movement.
Brandom's inferentialism directly develops Sellars's distinction between the space of reasons and the space of causes.
McDowell's Mind and World is in sustained dialogue with Sellars's "Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind."
Rorty was Sellars's student; Philosophy and the Mirror of Nature develops Sellarsian themes.
Brandom was Rorty's student at Princeton.
Mill was educated as a Benthamite but revised utilitarianism to distinguish quality of pleasures.
"Modern Moral Philosophy" called for return to Aristotelian virtue ethics.
Natural Goodness grounds ethics in Aristotelian natural teleology.
After Virtue diagnoses modernity's loss of the Aristotelian teleological framework.
Anarchy, State, and Utopia is written as an explicit libertarian response to A Theory of Justice.
Williams critiques Rawlsian moral theory for its detachment from actual human motivations.
Nagel's "What Is It Like to Be a Bat?" directly anticipates Chalmers's hard problem.
Searle was Austin's student; Speech Acts develops Austinian speech act theory systematically.
